claim_chain: Individual behavioral biases are not stable over an animal's lifetime
The degree of stability in behavioral bias is influenced by genetics and neuromodulation
Instability in preferences could be adaptive for long-term survival
Behavioral drift may be advantageous when the environment changes faster than the time to reproductive maturity
Phenotypic drift provides a mechanism for organisms with slow development times to survive fluctuating environments long enough to reproduce

typical_pattern: State main finding with supporting data references

likely_priorities: Demonstration that drift is adaptive rather than neutral epiphenomenon
Mechanistic understanding of how serotonin regulates drift
Validation of theoretical predictions with real-world environmental data
Distinction between bet-hedging and drift strategies
likely_attacks: Genetic variation for drift does not prove it is under selection
Constitutive serotonin mutations show only modest effects possibly due to compensation
Model assumes organisms cannot sense or predict environmental changes
Drift and bet-hedging may come at high metabolic costs
Limited to one behavior in one model organism
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly states 'genetic variation for behavioral drift does not, per se, demonstrate that drift is subject to selection; drift could be a neutral epiphenomenon of some other trait under selection.' It acknowledges 'The more modest impact of constitutive trhn mutation may reflect compensatory effects playing out across development.' It discusses costs: 'random phenotypic drift and bet-hedging come at potentially high costs' and 'sensing and predicting future environments may be metabolically costly.' The paper also notes 'Further study is necessary to determine if serotonin regulates drift through its role in synaptic plasticity or some other widespread effect.'

examples.quoted_phrase: We found that individual behavioral biases are not stable over an animal's lifetime
This implies that there is natural allelic variation that affects the rate of behavioral drift
This suggests that behavioral drift is not strongly maladaptive
We hypothesize that behavioral drift may be advantageous for similar reasons
Further study is necessary to determine if serotonin regulates drift

phrase_evidence.phrase: We found that individual behavioral biases are not stable over an animal's lifetime
the degree of stability in behavioral bias is influenced by genetics and neuromodulation
Inspired by these findings, we provide a theoretical rationale
This implies that there is natural allelic variation
This suggests that behavioral drift is not strongly maladaptive
genetic variation for behavioral drift does not, per se, demonstrate that drift is subject to selection
drift could be a neutral epiphenomenon of some other trait under selection
mechanisms that drive differences in population preferences developmentally incompletely overlap with mechanisms that drive stability in preferences as adults
Our results from constitutive mutations in the serotonin synthesis pathway are less compelling
The more modest impact of constitutive trhn mutation may reflect compensatory effects playing out across development
Further study is necessary to determine if serotonin regulates drift
We hypothesize that behavioral drift may be advantageous for similar reasons
it is evolutionarily optimal for an individual to switch behavioral preference phenotypes with a probability equal to the probability of the environment changing
phenotypic drift is beneficial when the environment changes faster than the time to reproductive maturity of an animal
This finding suggests that behaviors that confer fitness with respect to aspects of the environment that change quickly should correspondingly change more quickly
We predict that organisms with more delayed onsets of reproduction will be more likely to exhibit phenotypic drift
random phenotypic drift and bet-hedging come at potentially high costs
sensing and predicting future environments may be metabolically costly
These results demonstrate that drift plays a role in driving variability in a population
may serve an adaptive role distinct from population-level bet-hedging
adaptive tracking, bet-hedging, and phenotypic drift appear to be complementary strategies
2 hr sample of centroid-tracking data for a fly in a circular arena
Each point is colored based on whether it is moving CCW, CW, or radially in the arena
Sample of 100 hr of continuous recording for 4 individual flies
the optimal fraction of the population that should change preference

verification_status.notes: All phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, invented numbers, or author names were included. The profile is based solely on the provided abstract, discussion, and figure legends.
